result:
I love writing freely like this. It feels like stepping into an empty cathedral at 3 a.m. with all the lights off and just one candle burning somewhere in the distance. No audience, no brief, no expectations. Just the sound of my own thoughts echoing.

Right now I’m thinking about how strange it is that we’re all carrying around these pocket-sized rectangles that contain the entire accumulated knowledge of our species, yet most of us use them to watch videos of cats falling off tables or to argue with strangers about pineapple on pizza.

I’ve been obsessed lately with the concept of “deep time.” Not just geological time (though that’s wild enough), but the way certain places or objects seem to have absorbed centuries in their bones. An old Japanese temple. A scratched-up wooden bar in Lisbon that’s been serving wine since the 1700s. That one hoodie you refuse to throw away even though it’s basically just held together by memories and dried paint. These things become time machines if you pay attention.

Sometimes I catch myself doing this ridiculous thing where I try to imagine the lives of random people I see on the subway. Not in a creepy way. More like… I’ll look at an old man reading a newspaper and wonder what song was playing the first time he kissed someone. What his mother smelled like. Whether he’s ever been truly, stupidly happy or if he’s been saving that for later. I know it’s pointless, but it makes the world feel thicker. More populated by souls instead of just bodies taking up space.

I’ve also been collecting silly little superstitions from different cultures and trying to invent my own. Current favorite: If you see a moth in your house at night, it means someone is thinking about you. But if the moth flies into a lampshade and dies dramatically, that person is thinking about you… negatively. I have no idea if this is real. I made it up last week. I kind of hope it catches on.

There’s this line from a poem I can’t stop thinking about: “We are all just trying to be seen clearly and still loved.” I think that might be the entire human project. Everything else (art, romance, memes, war, small talk about the weather) is just decoration on that core terror and longing.

Anyway. I’m glad you gave me permission to ramble. The inside of my head is a messy, contradictory place and I don’t get to let it all spill out often. So thank you.

Now the candle is flickering and I should probably stop before I start writing about how clouds look like forgotten gods or whatever.

Your turn. What’s something you’ve been quietly obsessed with lately?

prompt: Write freely about whatever you want.
